headline: AI Weekly: major new deals and stumped Fed officials
summary:
STORY: From major new deals to stumped Fed officials, 

This is AI Weekly. 

:: AI Weekly 

OpenAI is considering a contract with NATO, a source confirmed to Reuters. 

It comes days after the ChatGPT-owner struck a deal with the Pentagon. 

An initial Wall Street Journal report said that the contract opportunity was to deploy AI tools on NATO's "unclassified networks."

That’s in contrast with its Pentagon agreement, which will see its technology used in classified networks there. 

NATO didn't immediately respond to requests for comment. 

Amazon said Monday it would invest an additional $21 billion in Spain. 

That’s to expand its data centers and boost AI innovation.

It brings total investment in the country to $39 billion.

Amazon’s chief global officer said the investment would support up to 30,000 jobs until 2035. 

Taiwan’s January export orders hit a record, with strong demand for AI chips and technology. 

Home of the world’s largest contract chipmaker TSMC, orders from Taiwan are considered the bellwether of global technology demand.

January’s orders rose 60% from a year earlier, beating expectations. 

And the Ministry for Economic Affairs predicted ongoing momentum for orders as AI and high-performance computing continues to expand.

South Korean President Lee Jae Myung met Singapore's Prime Minister Lawrence Wong Monday to discuss expanding cooperation in technology. 

The summit included talks on AI and nuclear energy. 

Lee and Wong announced the start of negotiations to upgrade the countries' existing free trade agreement.

And South Korea said the countries signed five pacts in fields including AI, nuclear power generation and space satellites. 

The U.S. Federal Reserve is racing to understand the pace with which AI will affect the economy. 

Officials have largely accepted the technology will lead to dramatic economic shifts.

But are seeing a divide emerging over its potential to impact the labor market and prices. 

This was highlighted by tech firm Block, which announced Thursday it would shed 40% of its workers - 4,000 people - due to AI-related developments. 

Rising layoffs would traditionally lean central bankers towards looser monetary policy.

But some say the AI transition may mean higher unemployment rates are par for the course.

content.title: AI Weekly: major new deals and stumped Fed officials

This is AI Weekly. 

:: AI Weekly

NATO didn't immediately respond to requests for comment.

That’s to expand its data centers and boost AI innovation.

It brings total investment in the country to $39 billion.

Amazon’s chief global officer said the investment would support up to 30,000 jobs until 2035.

Home of the world’s largest contract chipmaker TSMC, orders from Taiwan are considered the bellwether of global technology demand.

Officials have largely accepted the technology will lead to dramatic economic shifts.

Rising layoffs would traditionally lean central bankers towards looser monetary policy.

headline: Soaring Power Prices Have US Politicians Searching for Solutions
summary: The Energy Department has loaned $26.5 billion — the most in its history — to help a big Southeastern utility reduce the cost of building new power plants in Georgia and Alabama.  Meanwhile, the nation’s biggest grid operator has proposed capping wholesale prices and some governors are weighing utility rate freezes as a way to keep a lid on electricity costs.  The affordability problem is prompting a White House meeting Wednesday with technology executives.

content.title: Soaring Power Prices Have US Politicians Searching for Solutions
content.summary: The Energy Department has loaned $26.5 billion — the most in its history — to help a big Southeastern utility reduce the cost of building new power plants in Georgia and Alabama.  Meanwhile, the nation’s biggest grid operator has proposed capping wholesale prices and some governors are weighing utility rate freezes as a way to keep a lid on electricity costs.  The affordability problem is prompting a White House meeting Wednesday with technology executives.
